title: The last Tuesday
summary: An Irish late-night radio show is being cancelled. The host phones — or rather, takes the phone call from — her most regular listener, on the second-to-last Tuesday.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Hello, you're on Late Light. Who's there?

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Mr Hennessy, Eileen. Mr Hennessy from Ballinasloe.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Mr Hennessy. Of course. It's Tuesday.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: It is Tuesday. I heard the announcement on Sunday.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: I was going to phone you.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: You don't have my number. Nobody has my number. I gave it once to a daughter and she lost it in 1998.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: How many years have you been calling, Mr Hennessy?

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Eighteen this autumn. The first time was October 2007. You played the song my wife liked. I called to thank you. You said I should call back the next week.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: I remember.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: You told me afterwards you were trying to fill thirty seconds at the end of the show.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: That's true. I had a gap.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: And here we are. Last show. Friday.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Friday.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Why are they closing it?

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Numbers. The station says only three hundred and ten people listen now. The last time there were fewer than five hundred, the management had a meeting. Then they had ten more meetings. Then the meetings stopped, which is how I knew.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: I never thought of myself as one of three hundred and ten people, Eileen.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: No. I never thought of you as one of three hundred and ten either. I thought of you as Mr Hennessy from Ballinasloe.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Will you tell me something honestly?

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Did the show keep going partly because I called?

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Yes.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Truly?

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Truly. Three years ago they wanted to cancel it. I told them I had a regular caller who had not missed a Tuesday since 2007 and that he was reason enough. They gave me three more years.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Eileen.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: I'm sorry, Mr Hennessy.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Do not be sorry. You gave me three years. I gave you eighteen. We are even.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: We are not even. We are not the same kind of friends. But we have known each other.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: We have known each other. Will I see you Friday?

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: You will see me on Friday.

body.speaker: Mr Hennessy
body.text: Goodnight, Eileen.

body.speaker: Eileen
body.text: Goodnight, Mr Hennessy.

annotations.meaningVi: người gọi đều / thường xuyên (đến chương trình)

annotations.phrase: I was going to phone you
annotations.meaningVi: 'was going to' diễn tả ý định trong quá khứ chưa thực hiện được
lấp đầy (khoảng thời gian) — collocation phổ biến trong ngữ cảnh truyền thông
và đây là kết quả — mệnh đề cố định diễn tả hệ quả không ngờ tới
tiếp tục duy trì / tiếp tục hoạt động (phrasal verb)
ít hơn (dùng với danh từ đếm được)
đó là cách tôi biết — mệnh đề quan hệ giải thích cách nhận ra thông tin

annotations.phrase: will you tell me something honestly
annotations.meaningVi: bạn có nói thật với tôi một điều không — cách yêu cầu sự thành thật lịch sự

annotations.phrase: I never thought of myself as
annotations.meaningVi: tôi chưa bao giờ nghĩ mình là — cụm cố định diễn tả sự tự nhìn nhận
huỷ bỏ chương trình — 'cancel' kết hợp phổ biến với kế hoạch, sự kiện, chương trình
đừng xin lỗi — cụm cố định từ chối sự thương hại hoặc lời xin lỗi
cùng kiểu bạn bè — cụm danh từ diễn tả mức độ hay bản chất của mối quan hệ
'afterwards' = sau đó (mốc thời gian tương đối, dùng như trạng từ diễn ra)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for shared time: 'have known each other', 'has not missed since 2007'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hiện tại hoàn thành ('have/has + V3') diễn tả việc bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn nối đến hiện tại — đặc biệt phù hợp để mô tả mối quan hệ kéo dài. Trong bài: 'How many years have you been calling?' (hành động lặp lại, vẫn diễn ra). 'A regular caller who had not missed a Tuesday since 2007.' ('had not missed' là quá khứ hoàn thành — vì câu kể về thời điểm 'three years ago'). 'We have known each other.' (đến giờ vẫn quen.) Lưu ý: 'have known' vẽ ra mối quan hệ; 'know' đơn thuần thì không tính thời gian.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect ('have/has + past participle') marks something that began in the past and connects to now — ideal for long relationships. 'How many years have you been calling?' (a repeating action, still going). 'A regular caller who had not missed a Tuesday since 2007.' (here 'had not missed' is past perfect — because the frame is 'three years ago'). 'We have known each other.' (still, now.) Plain present 'know' would lose the long timeline.

translationVi:
Eileen: Xin chào, bạn đang nghe Late Light. Ai đầu dây vậy ạ?
Ông Hennessy: Ông Hennessy đây, Eileen. Ông Hennessy ở Ballinasloe.
Eileen: Ông Hennessy. Tất nhiên rồi. Hôm nay là thứ Ba mà.
Ông Hennessy: Đúng thứ Ba. Tôi nghe thông báo hôm Chủ nhật.
Eileen: Tôi định gọi cho ông.
Ông Hennessy: Cô đâu có số tôi. Không ai có số tôi. Có lần tôi đưa cho đứa con gái, năm 1998 nó làm mất rồi.
Eileen: Ông gọi vào chương trình bao nhiêu năm rồi, ông Hennessy?
Ông Hennessy: Mùa thu này là mười tám. Lần đầu là tháng Mười năm 2007. Cô bật bài mà vợ tôi thích. Tôi gọi để cảm ơn. Cô bảo tôi tuần sau gọi lại.
Eileen: Tôi nhớ.
Ông Hennessy: Sau đó cô có nói với tôi rằng cô đang cần lấp ba mươi giây cuối chương trình.
Eileen: Đúng. Tôi có khoảng trống.
Ông Hennessy: Và giờ thì như thế này. Buổi cuối. Thứ Sáu.
Eileen: Thứ Sáu.
Ông Hennessy: Sao họ đóng chương trình?
Eileen: Số liệu. Đài nói giờ chỉ có ba trăm mười người nghe. Lần trước khi xuống dưới năm trăm, lãnh đạo họp một lần. Họ họp thêm mười lần nữa. Rồi các cuộc họp dừng — đó là cách tôi biết.
Ông Hennessy: Tôi chưa bao giờ nghĩ mình là một trong ba trăm mười người, Eileen.
Eileen: Tôi cũng chưa bao giờ nghĩ về ông như một trong ba trăm mười người. Tôi nghĩ về ông là ông Hennessy ở Ballinasloe.
Ông Hennessy: Cô nói thật với tôi một điều được không?
Eileen: Được.
Ông Hennessy: Có phải một phần chương trình duy trì được là vì tôi gọi?
Eileen: Có.
Ông Hennessy: Thật chứ?
Eileen: Thật. Ba năm trước họ muốn đóng. Tôi nói với họ tôi có một người gọi đều, không sót một thứ Ba nào từ 2007, và ông ấy là đủ lý do. Họ cho tôi thêm ba năm nữa.
Ông Hennessy: Eileen ơi.
Eileen: Tôi xin lỗi, ông Hennessy.
Ông Hennessy: Đừng xin lỗi. Cô cho tôi ba năm. Tôi cho cô mười tám. Hoà rồi.
Eileen: Không hoà đâu. Chúng ta không phải kiểu bạn giống nhau. Nhưng chúng ta đã quen nhau.
Ông Hennessy: Chúng ta đã quen nhau. Thứ Sáu cô có ở đó không?
Eileen: Thứ Sáu ông sẽ thấy tôi.
Ông Hennessy: Chúc Eileen ngủ ngon.
Eileen: Chúc ông ngủ ngon, ông Hennessy.

questions.explanation: 'Mr Hennessy, Eileen. Mr Hennessy from Ballinasloe.'

questions.prompt: How did he find out the show was being cancelled?
options: He heard the announcement on Sunday
He read it in the paper

questions.prompt: When did Mr Hennessy first call the show?
questions.hint: He gives the month and year.

questions.prompt: Why did he call the first time?
options: Eileen played the song his wife liked; he called to thank her
He had a question about the weather
questions.explanation: 'You played the song my wife liked. I called to thank you.'
questions.hint: Mr Hennessy's story of the first call.

questions.prompt: What did Eileen admit she was doing on that first call?
options: Trying to fill thirty seconds at the end of the show
questions.explanation: 'You told me afterwards you were trying to fill thirty seconds at the end of the show.' / 'That's true. I had a gap.'
questions.hint: Look at the exchange about the thirty seconds.

questions.prompt: How many people listen to the show now, according to the station?
questions.explanation: 'The station says only three hundred and ten people listen now.'

questions.prompt: How did Eileen know that the cancellation was decided?
options: An email was sent to all staff.
She read it on the website.
questions.explanation: 'Then the meetings stopped, which is how I knew.'
questions.hint: Eileen describes the sequence: meetings, then no meetings.

questions.prompt: What did Eileen tell management three years ago when they wanted to cancel?
options: She had a regular caller who had not missed a Tuesday since 2007 and he was reason enough.
She would move the show to mornings.
questions.explanation: 'I had a regular caller who had not missed a Tuesday since 2007 and that he was reason enough.'
questions.hint: Look at Eileen's reply about three years ago.

questions.prompt: What is Mr Hennessy's reply to 'I'm sorry, Mr Hennessy'?
options: 'Do not be sorry. You gave me three years. I gave you eighteen. We are even.'
questions.hint: Look at his reply about giving years to each other.

questions.prompt: How does Eileen describe their relationship?
options: Not even, not the same kind of friends, but they 'have known each other'
questions.explanation: 'We are not even. We are not the same kind of friends. But we have known each other.'
questions.hint: Look at her precise correction of 'even.'

questions.prompt: Why does Eileen reject the word 'even'?
options: She is naming the asymmetry — they were not the same kind of friends, even though they meant something to each other.
She is angry with Mr Hennessy.
questions.explanation: She rejects 'even' but still affirms 'we have known each other.'
questions.hint: Notice that 'even' assumes a perfect symmetry between very different roles.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A relationship between a host and a listener can be real and irreplaceable, without being symmetrical or fitting any standard label.
Late-night radio is too expensive to run.
Old people should call radio shows.
Eileen made a mistake by keeping the show on.
questions.explanation: The dialogue ends with 'We have known each other.' — a small, honest description of an unusual bond.
questions.hint: Notice what kind of friendship the dialogue names — and what it refuses to call it.

cloze.template:
Eileen: Hello, you're on Late Light. Who's there?
Mr Hennessy: Mr Hennessy, Eileen. Mr Hennessy from {{b1}}.
Eileen: Mr Hennessy. Of course. It's Tuesday.
Mr Hennessy: It is Tuesday. I heard the announcement on Sunday.
Eileen: I was going to phone you.
Mr Hennessy: You don't have my number. Nobody has my number. I gave it once to a daughter and she lost it in 1998.
Eileen: How many years have you been {{b2}}, Mr Hennessy?
Mr Hennessy: Eighteen this autumn. The first time was October {{b3}}. You played the song my wife liked. I called to thank you. You said I should call back the next week.
Eileen: I remember.
Mr Hennessy: You told me afterwards you were trying to fill {{b4}} seconds at the end of the show.
Eileen: That's true. I had a gap.
Mr Hennessy: And here we are. Last show. Friday.
Eileen: Friday.
Mr Hennessy: Why are they closing it?
Eileen: Numbers. The station says only three hundred and ten people {{b5}} now. The last time there were fewer than five hundred, the management had a meeting. Then they had ten more meetings. Then the meetings stopped, which is how I knew.
Mr Hennessy: I never thought of myself as one of three hundred and ten people, Eileen.
Eileen: No. I never thought of you as one of three hundred and ten either. I thought of you as Mr Hennessy from {{b6}}.
Mr Hennessy: Will you tell me something honestly?
Eileen: Yes.
Mr Hennessy: Did the show keep going partly because I {{b7}}?
Eileen: Yes.
Mr Hennessy: Truly?
Eileen: Truly. Three years ago they wanted to cancel it. I told them I had a regular caller who had not missed a Tuesday since 2007 and that he was reason {{b8}}. They gave me three more years.
Mr Hennessy: Eileen.
Eileen: I'm sorry, Mr Hennessy.
Mr Hennessy: Do not be sorry. You gave me three years. I gave you eighteen. We are {{b9}}.
Eileen: We are not even. We are not the same kind of friends. But we have {{b10}} each other.
Mr Hennessy: We have known each other. Will I see you Friday?
Eileen: You will see me on Friday.
Mr Hennessy: Goodnight, Eileen.
Eileen: Goodnight, Mr {{b11}}.

blanks.explanation: How many years have you been calling — present perfect continuous.
Three hundred and ten people listen.
Mr Hennessy from Ballinasloe — a specific person.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Eileen refuses Mr Hennessy's word 'even' because their roles were never equal — yet she still affirms the relationship. Is there someone in your life with whom the connection is real but not equal — a teacher, a barista, a neighbour? What would be lost if you tried to force it into a label like 'friends'?
